title: Optimal work extraction in measurement-based quantum Otto engines: Non-adiabaticity and generalized measurements can be beneficial
authors: Arunabha Das, Sayan Mondal, Debarupa Saha, Ujjwal Sen
abstract: Measurement-based quantum heat engines have attracted significant interest as alternatives to conventional thermal engines, as they replace the hot thermal reservoir with quantum measurements, thereby offering greater controllability and simpler implementation. Motivated by these advantages, we investigate a measurement-driven quantum Otto engine with a qubit working substance and study the optimal work extractable from such engines, including whether their performance can surpass that of conventional quantum Otto cycles. We analyze the engine in both the infinite-time (adiabatic) and finite-time (non-adiabatic) regimes, considering two distinct implementations obtained through optimization over all projection-valued measurements (PVMs) and over all two-outcome positive operator-valued measurements (POVMs). We show that measurement-based engines can outperform conventional quantum Otto engines within specific parameter regimes and that POVM-based engines can yield higher optimal work extraction than PVM-based ones. Furthermore, by incorporating the thermodynamic cost associated with resetting the auxiliary system required for POVM implementation, we demonstrate that the resulting net work output can still exceed that of PVM-based engines under suitable conditions on the spectral gaps and cold bath temperature. We also identify regimes in which non-adiabatic implementations can yield higher work output and efficiency than their adiabatic counterparts. Our study provides operational guidelines for designing improved measurement-driven quantum Otto engines.
summary: This paper explores how replacing a thermal reservoir with quantum measurements can optimize a quantum Otto engine. It finds that generalized measurements (POVMs) and non-adiabatic processes can increase work extraction and efficiency, even when the energy cost of resetting the measurement apparatus is considered.
main_problem: Investigating the optimal work extraction and efficiency of measurement-based quantum Otto engines, specifically comparing PVM and POVM implementations under adiabatic and non-adiabatic regimes.
main_result: POVM-based engines can outperform PVM-based engines even after accounting for the thermodynamic cost of resetting the auxiliary system, and non-adiabaticity can enhance both work output and efficiency in certain compression regimes.
method: Analytical optimization of measurement parameters and unitary operators, combined with numerical optimization using the dual annealing algorithm and Naimark's dilation theorem.
model_or_system: A single-qubit working substance undergoing a four-stroke Otto cycle, where the hot-bath thermalization is replaced by quantum measurements (PVM or POVM) using an auxiliary system.
key_observables: Extractable work, efficiency, heat exchange, and net work output after accounting for auxiliary reset costs.
important_parameters: Compression ratio (frequency ratio), non-adiabaticity parameter (transition probability), cold bath temperature, and measurement basis angles.
main_assumptions: The analysis is restricted to the non-inverted population regime and focuses on two-outcome POVMs implemented via a single auxiliary qubit.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the schematic of the measurement-based Otto cycle; Figure 2 plots extractable work versus non-adiabaticity; Figure 4 illustrates the trade-off between POVM work gain and auxiliary reset cost as a function of temperature.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the measurement-based engine model, analyzes the adiabatic limit for PVM and POVM, extends the analysis to the non-adiabatic regime, evaluates the thermodynamic cost of POVM implementation, and concludes with operational guidelines.
why_interesting: It provides fundamental insights into the thermodynamics of measurement-driven processes and demonstrates how controlling non-adiabaticity and measurement type can be used to optimize quantum heat engines.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Schematic diagram of the measurement-based quan- tum Otto engine. The measurement-based quantum Otto engine consists of four strokes. In Stroke-I, the working medium thermal- izes with a cold bath at temperature Tc. Stroke-II and Stroke-IV are unitary driving strokes, generated through adiabatic or non-adiabatic driving of the Hamiltonian. Since these strokes are entropy conserv- ing, they correspond to the work strokes of the cycle. Stroke-III is the measurement stroke, which replaces the hot-bath thermalization pro- cess present in the conventional Otto engine, where the system would otherwise interact with a bath at temperature Th > Tc. The measure- ment can be either PVM or...

Figure 2. Extractable work: comparison between the PVM-based and conventional two-bath Otto engines. The non-adiabatic work output, W, is plotted as a function of the non-adiabaticity parameter P, where P = 1 corresponds to the adiabatic limit. The dashed turquoise curve denotes the maximum extractable work from the PVM-based Otto engine, WΠ M, obtained after optimization over all measurement settings. For comparison, the work extracted from a conventional two-bath Otto engine is also shown for two hot-bath inverse temperatures: βh = 0.2 (dot-dashed blue) and βh = 0 (solid orange), represented by [WC]βh=0.2 and [WC]βh=0, respectively. Panels (a) and (b) correspond to two different...

Figure 3. Extractable work: comparison between the PVM-based and POVM-based Otto engines. The non-adiabatic work output, W, is plotted as a function of the non-adiabaticity parameter P, where P = 1 corresponds to the adiabatic limit. The turquoise dashed curve denotes the maximum extractable work from the POVM-based Otto engine, WP M, obtained after optimization over all measurement settings. At the adiabatic limit, i.e., P = 1, this curve touches with the analytically derived value, i.e., W P M, represented by the yellow-colored dotted horizontal line.
